abstract: The cleavage of carbon-halogen bonds in copper-catalyzed cross-coupling reactions has long remained enigmatic, primarily due to the diverse redox manifolds available to copper and the inherent instability of high-valent intermediates. Here, we report the oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitriles to ionic and neutral copper(I) complexes, resulting in the formation of previously elusive copper(III) species. These complexes were fully characterized, with their unexpected stability attributed to the robust Cu–CF₃ bond and a high kinetic barrier preventing C(CF₃)–C(CH₂CN) reductive elimination. Our mechanistic investigations reveal that oxidative addition follows distinct pathways depending on the precursor: an Sₙ2-type substitution occurs with ionic complexes, whereas neutral complexes proceed via halogen-atom transfer. Furthermore, we identified a significant ligand-acceleration effect during oxidative addition that directly correlates with the rates observed in the copper-catalyzed coupling of alkyl electrophiles with azoles, amines, and alkynes.
